key_insight: In rough, load-bearing lubricated contacts, a two-fluid lubricant outperforms single-component water or n-dodecane by sustaining plug flow longer and minimizing friction/material transfer, while roughness-induced confinement can trigger plug flow and cavitation.
engineering_relevance: The results suggest that lubricant chemistry and immiscibility can be used to improve boundary/mixed-lubrication performance in real rough contacts, not just idealized smooth interfaces.
method: Non-equilibrium molecular dynamics of sliding between rough, deformable surfaces.
Compared three lubricants under identical geometry: water, n-dodecane, and an immiscible two-fluid system.
Analyzed friction, load-bearing behavior, plug flow, cavitation, and wear/material transfer.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports direct simulation comparisons showing water has higher speed dependence but lower load-bearing capacity than n-dodecane, and the mixed lubricant gives the lowest friction and transfer.
watch_item: The findings come from molecular-scale simulations under specific rough-contact geometries, so translating the performance gains to macroscopic engineering systems will require validation.
paper_title: Plug Flow and Cavitation in Rough Lubricated Contacts: Molecular Dynamics of Single- vs. Two-Component Fluids
